title: Read capability and preference signals
overview: Extend the existing BrowserSignals interface and readSignals() function in src/input/signals.ts to include hardware concurrency, color scheme preference, reduced motion preference, and touch capability. All values remain in memory only and are treated as optional.

files_to_modify.description: Extend BrowserSignals interface with 4 new fields and update readSignals() to read them
Add test cases for each new capability/preference signal, including fallback behavior

implementation_steps.detail: Add 4 new optional fields to the existing interface: hardwareConcurrency (number | null), prefersColorScheme ('light' | 'dark' | null), prefersReducedMotion (boolean | null), touchCapable (boolean | null). Using null for unavailable values keeps the pattern consistent with the existing devicePixelRatio field.
Read navigator.hardwareConcurrency if available, return null otherwise. Use nullish coalescing: navigator.hardwareConcurrency ?? null.

Use window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches and window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: light)').matches to determine preference. Return 'dark', 'light', or null if matchMedia is unavailable or neither matches. Wrap in try/catch for environments where matchMedia is undefined.
Use window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches to detect reduced motion preference. Return true if matches, false if not, null if matchMedia is unavailable. Wrap in try/catch.
Detect touch support using: 'ontouchstart' in window || navigator.maxTouchPoints > 0. Return boolean result, or null if neither API is available. No permission prompts required.
Update the test that asserts 'returns object with exactly the expected keys' to include the 4 new keys in the sorted expected array.
Test cases: (a) returns navigator.hardwareConcurrency when available (mock to 8), (b) returns null when hardwareConcurrency is undefined.
Test cases: (a) returns 'dark' when dark scheme matches, (b) returns 'light' when light scheme matches, (c) returns null when matchMedia is unavailable. Mock window.matchMedia to control results.
Test cases: (a) returns true when reduced motion is preferred, (b) returns false when no preference, (c) returns null when matchMedia is unavailable.
Test cases: (a) returns true when ontouchstart exists in window, (b) returns true when maxTouchPoints > 0, (c) returns false when neither is available.

implementation_steps.action: Write privacy tests for new signals
implementation_steps.detail: Verify that reading capability/preference signals does not access localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, or trigger any permission prompts. The existing privacy tests cover this partially but confirm no regressions.

implementation_steps.action: Verify build and all tests pass
implementation_steps.detail: Run npm run build and npm run test to confirm no TypeScript errors, no test failures, and no console errors.

testing_strategy.approach: Extend existing tests/signals.test.ts with a new describe block for US-004. Mock navigator and window properties using vi.spyOn and Object.defineProperty. Test both presence and absence of each API.

acceptance_criteria_mapping.Read hardwareConcurrency if available: Step 2 — reads navigator.hardwareConcurrency with null fallback
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Read prefers-color-scheme: Step 3 — uses matchMedia to detect color scheme preference
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Read prefers-reduced-motion: Step 4 — uses matchMedia to detect reduced motion preference
acceptance_criteria_mapping.All values stay in memory only: No storage APIs used; verified by existing and new privacy tests

risks.mitigation: Mock window.matchMedia in tests; implementation wraps calls in try/catch

risks.risk: Existing test asserting exact keys will fail until updated
risks.mitigation: Step 6 updates the key-check test before running the full suite

definition_of_done: No console errors on normal load
All 4 new signals readable when APIs available
All 4 new signals gracefully null when APIs unavailable
